coredata.dc:title: Electron transport of chemically treated graphene quantum dots-based dye-sensitized solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Mahalingam, Savisha
Manap, Abreeza
Rabeya, Ramisha
Lau, Kam Sheng
Chia, Chin Hua
Abdullah, Huda
Amin, Nowshad
Chelvanathan, Puvaneswaran
coredata.dc:description: The main aim of this work is to elucidate the effect of the chemical treatment of titanium tetrachloride (TiCl4) in graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-based dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). Although this type of chemical treatment has been used in DSSCs, the detailed electron transport properties of TiCl4-treated TiO2/GQDs+dye have not been studied yet. In this study, we have proposed a detailed mechanism for how the extra titania layer with TiCl4 provides high surface area and porosity to improve the adsorption of GQDs and dye. Moreover, the electron transport analysis revealed that the treatment reduced the electron recombination rate and increased the electron injection efficiency up to 73.8%, leading to a high Jsc and longer electron lifetime in the DSSC. Additionally, a performance comparison study with other types of GQDs under TiCl4 treatment was also investigated in this work.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electron injection efficiency
Energy
Graphene quantum dots
Titanium tetrachloride

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Performance comparison with other types of GQDs-based DSSC

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank Universiti Tenaga Nasional (UNITEN) for funding this study under the UNITEN BOLD of Grant No. J510050002/2022002 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic structure of TiCl4 treatment in GQDs-based dye-sensitized solar cell.
FESEM images of (a) without TiCl4 and corresponding size distribution and (b) without TiCl4 and corresponding size distribution.
Raman plots of with TiCl4, and the inserted image shows the zoomed region of without TiCl4 and TiO2/TiCl4.
FT-IR plots of with TiCl4, without TiCl4, and TiO2/TiCl4.
UV–vis plots of with TiCl4, without TiCl4, and TiO2/TiCl4.
PL plots of bare sensitizer (N719+GQDs), with TiCl4, and without TiCl4.
Proposed mechanism of TiCl4 treatment on TiO2 surface.
J-V curves of samples with TiCl4 and without TiCl4.
EIS plots, corresponding bode plots and circuit models of (a) treated cell (b) untreated cell (c) combined EIS plots for the treated and untreated cells.
Cyclic voltammetry curves for GQDs-based DSSC devices with and without TiCl4 treatment.
J-V plots of other types of GQDs with and without TiCl4 treatment in DSSC.
BET parameters of treated and untreated samples.
J-V parameters of TiCl4 treated and untreated DSSCs.
EIS data of the treated and untreated cell.
J-V parameters of other types of GQDs with and without TiCl4 treatment in DSSC.

head.ce:title: Electron transport of chemically treated graphene quantum dots-based dye-sensitized solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical, Electronic and Systems Engineering, Faculty of Engineering and Built Environment, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, Bangi, Selangor 43600, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Solar Energy Research Institute, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, Bangi, Selangor 43600, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: eSolar Energy Research Institute, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, Bangi, Selangor 43600, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The main aim of this work is to elucidate the effect of the chemical treatment of titanium tetrachloride (TiCl4) in graphene quantum dots (GQDs)-based dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). Although this type of chemical treatment has been used in DSSCs, the detailed electron transport properties of TiCl4-treated TiO2/GQDs+dye have not been studied yet. In this study, we have proposed a detailed mechanism for how the extra titania layer with TiCl4 provides high surface area and porosity to improve the adsorption of GQDs and dye. Moreover, the electron transport analysis revealed that the treatment reduced the electron recombination rate and increased the electron injection efficiency up to 73.8%, leading to a high Jsc and longer electron lifetime in the DSSC. Additionally, a performance comparison study with other types of GQDs under TiCl4 treatment was also investigated in this work.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) made the first breakthrough in 1991 by O'Regan and Gratzel with low-cost fabrication, a simple manufacturing process and high photovoltaic efficiency [1]. Owing to that, they are more desirable than silicon solar cells. They are among the third-generation solar cells that offer several benefits over silicon solar cells due to their low dependency on light intensity and less sensitivity to the angle of incidence [2]. Moreover, there are other classifications of DSSCs, such as fiber DSSCs, which are flexible, lightweight, inexpensive, and easily fabricated and deformed. These make them beneficial for being effectively applicable in wearable electronics and can also sustainably supply power [3]. However, despite the DSSC advantages, the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of DSSCs undergoing chemical treatment and nanoparticle fabrication is still low. This is because of several issues, such as instability and toxicity, which could significantly reduce the performance of DSSC. Moreover, one of the significant shortcomings of DSSC lies in its narrow absorption of the solar spectrum, which leads to high charge recombination and low electron injection efficiency. Therefore, in order to enhance the electron injection efficiency, charge recombination is supposed to be mitigated [4].

To tackle this issue, a 2D graphene derivative, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can be a promising material for generating better PCE. 2D materials have outstanding optical properties, which makes them expedient for applications such as photonic devices, photodetectors, sensors, and lasers [5]. Furthermore, carbon nanostructure can be used to enhance conversion efficiency to modify a solar cell's photoanode. One known type of such material is graphene, favoured due to its outstanding characteristics. However, it comprises zero band gaps. Thus, this leads to the introduction of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as they are nontoxic, followed by having stable photoluminescence (PL), good solubility, and biocompatibility [6]. Furthermore, their explicit chemiluminescence, photoluminescence, fluorescence lead to extensive applications such as biochemical sensing [7], bioimaging, photovoltaic devices [6] etc. Ponomarenko and the Nobel Prize winners, Geim and Novoselov produced the first GQDs in 2008 via electron lithography [8].

Before moving on to further discussions on GQDs, graphene, graphene-based metals and metal oxides and their corresponding applications are the essential factors to take into account. Zang et al. [9] incorporated graphene oxide (GO) with AgInZnS water-soluble quantum dots forming an AIZS-GO composite which exhibited enhanced photoluminescent intensity compared to the composite without the presence of GO. This potential composite has been applied in-vivo bioimaging, remarkably proving biocompatibility, photo-stability and low bio toxicity. Furthermore, Liu et al. [10] reported on the execution of reduced graphene oxide (rGO) on CsBrPbBr3 nanoparticles, which eventually formed CsBrPbBr3/rGO nanocomposite that efficiently aided in the enhancement of photon absorption and charge carrier transport owing to the nanoparticles being evenly distributed onto rGO. This led to enhanced x-ay and optical photon response. The as-mentioned photon has been implemented to monitor the switching mechanism of a photoconductive device. Besides, a nitrogen dioxide sensor has been introduced by Ding et al. [11], who executed the composition of molybdenum disulfide (MoS2), rGO and copper (II) oxide (Cu2O) to fabricate the sensor forming MoS2-rGOCu2O nanocomposite synthesized via a hydrothermal and soft-template method. The incorporation of Cu2O/rGO led to improved sensing performance of the sensor.

The fascinating properties of GQDs, such as non-zero bandgap and photoluminescence (PL) upon excitation, provide high compatibility to be used in DSSCs. Based on tuning the size of GQDs and suitable functionalization, the energy band alignment between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) in GQDs can be altered, as proved by Chakravarti et al. [12] over a wide range of wavelengths [6,7]. Interestingly, GQDs can store charges, suggesting an inhabitation in electron recombination in DSSCs [13]. Additionally, they are capable of tuning the bandgap [14]. GQDs can eventually aid in utilizing sunlight optimally in PV cells. They can convert ultraviolet light (UV) to visible light in the active region of solar cells. Here, the lack of deep penetration into the cell results in the misusage of ultraviolet light. GQDs can broaden the solar spectrum absorption due to their aptitude for converting UV light to visible light. Hence more sunlight can be accumulated by the PV cell, which is devoted to higher conversion efficiency [15].

The abilities of GQDs to act as donors [16], which controls the internal recombination in the cell, and light absorbers [17] have been making their implementation worthy in PV cells since 2010. All the fundamental components of DSSCs, including photoelectrodes [18], light absorbers/sensitizers [10,12,13–21], electrolytes [22] and counter electrodes [23–28] have been associated with GQDs by researchers. The function of GQDs as light absorbers has been exploited by many researchers, who co-sensitized GQDs with various dyes [14–18]. The dye acts as both a photon acceptor from the sun and a hole acceptor from the HOMO of GQDs. Hence, GQDs can assist actively in electron injection, which prevents the recombination of electrons back into the dye hole by supporting charge separation and collection. Nevertheless, it is poor in GQDs-based DSSCs that influences low short-circuit current density, Jsc.

GQDs-based DSSCs usually produce low Jsc because of more significant charge recombination in the cell. Long et al. [29] reported that the physisorption process is more ideal compared to the chemisorption process for applying onto the TiO2 surface owing to the strong covalent attachment within the photoelectrode and GQDs [22]. Thus, we added TiCl4 treatment on top of the TiO2 electrode via the chemisorption process to reduce the charge recombination in GQDs-based DSSC. Several methods are being implemented for making TiCl4 as a blocking layer of TiO2 [30], such as spin-coating, spray pyrolysis, electrochemical deposition, etc. [31]. This blocking layer serves in condensing the charge recombination in the cell, which modifies the photoinjected electrons transport into TiO2 [32,33] and expands the specific surface area [34] of TiO2 followed by dye adsorption on the TiO2 surface [32,35], thus promoting greater Jsc [36].

Based on the previous studies [37,29], 40 mM is the most optimal TiCl4 concentration for treating the TiO2 layer for 30 min duration for 70 °C temperatures. This is evident in terms of enhancing the PV parameters of DSSC. Moreover, this treatment is beneficial in reducing unwanted recombination followed by increasing the lifetime of electrons injected into TiO2 from dye, which aids in enhancing the overall cell performance [37,29]. However, the detailed electron transport properties of TiCl4-treated TiO2/GQDs+dye have not been studied yet. In this study, we have reported a detailed analysis of how the extra titania layer with TiCl4 provides high surface area and porosity to improve the adsorption of GQDs and dye. This event will reduce the electron recombination rate and increase the electron injection efficiency leading to a high Jsc in the DSSC. Although the overall PCE of the DSSC in this work is low compared to the other works, this study may help researchers to have an in-depth electron transport analysis of the chemical treatment of TiCl4 for GQDs-based DSSC. Moreover, a performance comparison was conducted to investigate the effect of TiCl4 treatment on other types of GQDs via cyclic voltammetry (CV) and current density-voltage (J-V) curves.

The TiO2 paste was prepared by adding 0.2 g of P25 powder with 0.7 g of polyethylene glycol and adding 0.3 mL of ethanol. The solution was ground continuously for 10 min until a viscous paste was observed. Next, the TiO2 photoanodes were prepared using the doctor-blade technique on FTO glasses with 1 cm2 active area and dried in an oven for 30 min at 100°C. Then the TiO2 coated glasses were sintered at 450°C for 1 h using a tube furnace.

The TiCl4 treatment was conducted using TiCl4 with 1.0 M in toluene (Sigma-Aldrich USA). The prepared TiO2 electrodes were immersed in the benzene solution for 30 min. Finally, the treated TiO2 photoanodes were sintered at 450°C for 30 min and cooled to 80°C.

The synthesis process of GQDs has been carried out, followed by our previous work [38]. 0.0210 g of cellulose and 25 mL of distilled water were mixed via ultrasonication for 30 min. The mixture then underwent hydrothermal synthesis for 24 h and centrifugation at 1200 rpm for 30 min. Finally, a colourless GQDs solution was obtained. The same procedure was carried out using glucose and fructose instead of cellulose to obtain the GQDs solution.

After the TiCl4 treatment, the photoanodes were subjected to the immersion of photosensitizers. First, the photoanodes were immersed in a mixture of N719 and GQDs (N719+GQDs) for 24 h. Simultaneously, platinum-coated counter electrodes were prepared using the screen-printing technique. Next, the immersed electrodes are washed with ethanol to remove the excess GQDs and dye solution. The fabrication was done by sandwiching the immersed and counter electrodes using binder clips. Finally, an electrolyte (Iodolyte MPN 100) was injected into the sandwiched cell. The area of the electrode was 0.36 cm2. Fig. 1 shows the schematic structure of TiCl4 treatment in GQDs-based DSSCs.

The morphological properties were analyzed via FESEM (Merlin, Gemini II; 20 V-30 kV). The structural properties of the samples were analyzed by a confocal Raman Microscopy (Technospex uRaman-Ci; Nikon Eclipse Ci L laser microscope). The optical characterization was carried out using UV–vis (Perkin Elmer Lambda 35) and PL (Perkin Elmer Lambda 35). BET analysis was carried out to measure the specific surface area by adsorbing N2 gas molecules on the thin film surface. The test method of BET utilizes the static volumetric principle with gas-flowing technology. The extension of Langmuir's theory was used as the calculation model to determine the surface area, pore volume, and pore size. The chemical characterization was carried out using FTIR (Agilent Technologies; Cary 630). The CV and J-V characteristics were measured under a standard illumination level of 100 mW/cm2 via AAA class solar simulator (SSPN-X150T; Light Doctor Optical Corporation, Taiwan) IVDN-250E source meter. Three different conditions were investigated in this work: 1. TiO2/TiCl4; 2. TiO2/N719+GQDs (without TiCl4); 3. TiO2/TiCl4/N719+GQDs (with TiCl4). Moreover, a total of five samples were tested for each condition for all characterizations.

Fig. 2 shows the FESEM images and their corresponding particle size distributions of the treated and untreated samples. Both samples possess a mixture of small and large particles. However, the treated sample consists of more significant numbers of larger-sized particles. The untreated sample possesses an average diameter of approximately 74 nm. On the other hand, the treated sample exhibited a larger average diameter of 124 nm. Since they are treated with TiCl4, it adds an extra titania layer, increasing the porosity, as seen from the BET results discussed in Section 3.4.

Fig. 3 demonstrates the Raman plots for TiO2 with and without TiCl4 treatment. The peaks at 146 and 519 cm−1 correspond to Ti-O bonding in TiO2 [39]. After the sample was treated with TiCl4, two extra peaks were observed at 400 cm−1 and 637 cm−1, corresponding to the presence of TiCl4 ascribed to the TiO2-TiCl4 bond [31,40]. Moreover, the peaks at around 1272, 1475, 1538, and 1609 cm−1 belong to the N719 adsorption on the TiO2 surface, whereby the Raman intensity of these peaks shows increment for the TiCl4 treated TiO2 sample [40]. Furthermore, there are two graphitic features: D mode and G mode, which can be observed for the treated and untreated samples at 1315–1335 cm−1 and 1595 cm−1, respectively. The possible causes of these two modes can be symmetry breaking by defects and edges and in-plane CC deformation. These consequences confirm the presence of GQDs in the sample.

FTIR identifies the structure of chemical groups present on the surface of TiO2 samples, as shown in Fig. 4 . With TiCl4 treatment, the formation of titanium metal complexes can be confirmed from the peaks observed within 800–1000 cm−1, which corresponds to the Ti-O-Ti bond [34,41]. The O=C=O stretching bond is observed around 2400 cm−1 for all the samples. However, CH bending is observed for both the treated at around 1450 cm−1 and untreated samples, indicating the presence of GQDs, which is not observable in TiO2/TiCl4. Moreover, it is stronger in the case of the treated sample, which provides an estimated idea of the stronger adsorption of GQDs and TiO2 due to TiCl4 treatment. The existence of bound water on the surface of TiO2 samples gives rise to the broad peak observed at around 3430 cm−1, where the peak at 2950 cm−1 corresponds to the vibration mode of a hydroxyl (OH) group present in the carboxylic group of N719 molecules [42].

The TiCl4 treated TiO2 shows a decrease in absorbance and slightly shifted lower value for the OH peak could be due to the deprotonation of the COOH group in N719 molecules to COO− that have a better attachment on the TiO2 surface. The treated sample observed CH stretching in N719 molecules and GQDs at around 2850–3000 cm−1 [43]. Furthermore, the vibrational peaks observed at 1647 and 1399 cm−1 correspond to the presence of carboxylic (COOH) in N719 molecules and GQDs [38,39] in the treated sample.

The UV–vis spectrum of TiO2 with and without TiCl4 treatment is displayed in Fig. 5 . The TiO2/TiCl4 sample exhibits the highest absorbance concerning the wavelength above 300 nm, whereas the untreated sample possessed the lowest absorbance in the range of UV light around 250 nm. Besides, the absorption peaks at around 320, 400, and 500 nm corresponding to the N719 dye can be observed for the TiCl4 treated sample, which implies the increase of dye adsorption on the surface of TiO2 [44]. The BET analysis in Section 3.4 supports this event. The morphological changes in the TiCl4 treatment improve the surface properties following the FESEM observation. Thus, more photosensitizers (dye and GQDs) attach well to the treated TiO2 layer, which results in improved photocurrent and enhanced PV performance.

The surface area and particle size of treated and untreated samples were determined by analyzing the BET measurement. Table 1 shows the surface area, pore volume, and pore size of the treated and untreated samples. The incorporation of TiCl4 increases the titania layer's surface area and pore size. Adhikari et al. [45] mentioned that introducing TiCl4 decreases the crystalline grain and particle size, eventually increasing the pore size. In addition, a larger pore size leads to higher porosity in the photoanode that generates more electrons from an incident solar photon to increase the PCE of DSSCs [46]. Furthermore, a large surface area enables the treated titania layer to adsorb more dye and GQDs on the surface, increasing the Jsc [3] according to the FESEM images in Fig. 2.

Fig. 6 shows the PL emission of bare sensitizer (N719+GQDs) with TiCl4, and without TiCl4 samples. Due to the radiative emission, the bare sensitizer exhibits a more significant PL emission compared to the TiO2 photoanodes (with and without TiCl4). In order to determine the electron injection efficiency from sensitizer to CB of TiO2 of both the treated and untreated samples, the intensity of bare sensitizer with that of the TiO2 photoanodes was compared for each sample as in the following equation [47].
(1)
Electron
injection
efficiency
=
P
L
s e n s i t i z e r
−
P
L
T i O 2
p h o t o a n o d e
P
L
s e n s i t i z e r
×
100 %
With
TiC
l 4
=
, 500 , 00 − , 131 , 00
, 500 , 00
×
100 % = 73.8 %
Without
TiC
l 4
=
, 500 , 00 − , 444 , 00
, 500 , 00
×
100 % = 11.2 %

The PL intensities of 13,100, 44,400, and 50,000 were chosen as they are the highest intensities emitted at the visible region for each sample. The electron injection efficiency is determined by the amount of electrons injected from the dye to the CB of TiO2. If all the electrons get injected, 100% electron injection efficiency is attained, producing a graph with no peak. On the other hand, if they recombine with holes, then 0% efficiency occurs when the same PL intensity is acquired for bare dye. Here comes the idea of PL quenching, which is equivalent to the rate of electron injection efficiency. Greater the electron injection efficiency, the greater the PL quenching. Hence, for the TiCl4 treated sample, injection of more photoexcited electrons from the sensitizer to the CB of TiO2 occurs, enhancing the photocurrent [48] and electron injection efficiency [42]. As a result, the treated sample exhibited the highest PL quenching, followed by 73.8% of electrons being injected into the CB of TiO2, and thus, the rest 26.2%, recombined with the hole. So, lower PL quenching is identified for the sample without TiCl4 due to lower electron injection efficiency as relatively 62.6% lesser than the treated sample.

This can be due to the trap states between the LUMO of dye and CB of TiO2, which lets fewer electrons inject into the CB of TiO2, slowing the rate and leading to lower electron injection efficiency. Therefore, this confirms that adding TiCl4 results in a significant performance boost in electron injection efficiency, ensuring better adsorption between the photosensitizers and TiO2 surface.

The proposed mechanism of TiCl4 treatment of TiO2 surface is shown in Fig. 7 . The exposed surface of TiO2 (blue region) has a lot of OH groups bonded to the Ti atom in the inner part (gray) of the nanoparticle. The exposed OH group can react with the TiCl4 molecule and then by reacting with the H2O molecules, which form an extended unit of the –Ti(OH)3 group on the surface of TiO2. Thus, the formation of the extra layer of titania on the TiO2 nanoparticle can increase the particle size (as observed in the FESEM image) and porosity of the surface (as tabulated in Table 1) and may improve the adsorption of GQDs and N719 molecules on the TiO2 surface. When the GQDs and N719 molecules absorb photons, the electron from LUMO will be excited to HOMO, and then the electrons are readily injected into the TiO2 photoanode. This event will increase the electron injection efficiency in the DSSC.

The J-V characteristics of treated and untreated samples have been determined, as seen in Fig. 8 . The J-V parameters of the samples are given in Table 2 . It has been found that the sample containing TiCl4 exhibited the highest efficiency of 2.0% compared to the untreated one correlating that TiCl4 leads to a significant enhancement in electrical conductivity. The Voc and FF did not show significant improvements under TiCl4 influence as both samples possess almost the same Voc and FF. Almost 63% improvement was observed in the Jsc of the treated sample than that of the untreated DSSC.

The high Jsc is due to the fact that more dye is absorbed owing to the greater surface area in the photoanode of the treated sample, as identified from the BET result. Thus, it results in high porosity, as evident from the FESEM output. This expands the area for electrons to flow efficiently, which triggers faster electron flow, greatly quenches the PL, and ultimately improves the Jsc, as the absorption of light enhances in the semiconductor interface [49]. Therefore, greater PL quenching takes place. This Jsc improvement result matches the electron injection efficiency obtained through the PL result. Higher Jsc leads to greater electron flow, which is evident from the electron lifetime obtained for the treated sample. It is shorter than the untreated sample (80 ms), and the corresponding calculation and analysis have been discussed in the forthcoming section. Owing to the shorter effective electron lifetime (τeff), a greater number of electrons flow faster into the TiO2 [14], which eventually results in higher Jsc.

There is a strong correlation between photosensitizer adsorption and photocurrent, as it is proportional to the number of adsorptions of photosensitizer molecules on the TiO2 surface [43]. Furthermore, the high Jsc in the treated sample indicates that more electrons have been injected into the CB of TiO2 [50]. Therefore, more incident light is being harvested due to a more significant number of dye molecules attached, which results in a larger photocurrent leading to greater PCE [51]. Hence, TiCl4 treatment can be considered a source to improve the adsorption between the TiO2 surface and GQDs as the photosensitizer.

In order to verify the mechanism and the interface of TiCl4 treatment, electron impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis has been conducted. It has been carried out for both treated and untreated GQDs. The Nyquist plots with the corresponding bode plots and circuit models for the fabricated treated and untreated cells are illustrated in Fig. 9 (a) and (b) with their corresponding equivalent circuit models. The combined plots for the two cells are shown in Fig. 9(c). The radius of the middle semicircle arc denoted as the photoanode of the untreated sample is much greater than that of the treated sample. This indicates that the charge transfer resistance (Rct) for the untreated cell is higher, which means that the electron flow is minimum and is greatly resisted by the components of the DSSC. Rct also refers to the recombination resistance [14].

Furthermore, the electron lifetime (τeff) and effective rate constant (keff) are calculated based on previous studies [14] as in the following equations.
(2)
τ eff
=
1
2 ∏ fmax
(3)
k eff
=
1
τ eff
where, fmax is the maximum frequency of the EIS spectra. The electron transport properties of the fitted EIS data are listed in Table 3 . The fitted curve (as shown in Fig. 9) provides the electron transport parameters such as τeff and keff.

A previous study [52] shows that a large Rct in DSSC exhibits a facile electron transport. The untreated sample possesses a lower Rct (405.8 Ω) than the treated sample, causing a huge electron recombination rate (keff = 12.5 s−1). This result indicates the poor efficiency obtained through J-V analysis. The treated sample has the highest Rct of 1306.5 Ω, which can decrease the cell's recombination rate with a low keff of 5 s−1. The low electron recombination rate increased the Jsc in the TiCl4-treated GQDs-based DSSC (Fig. 8). According to the Rct values, higher charge transport has been attained for the treated sample, correlated to the higher charge injection efficiency obtained from the PL result. Greater electron injection efficiency means more electron gets injected into the CB of TiO2. Moreover, the greater electron injection efficiency in the treated sample is identified by the effective electron lifetime (τeff). A longer electron lifetime is exhibited for the treated samples with 200 ms. Greater τeff indicates that the electron recombination is suppressed [14], and faster electron injection has led to greater photocurrent flow [14].

In addition, the stability and linearity of the DSSCs, as presented by the Kronig-Kramers results, depend on the chi-square values of χ2 (Z), χ2 (Z’), and χ2 (-Z”). Whereas χ2 lower than 10−6 indicates an excellent fit, a reasonable fit between 10−5 and 10−6, a marginal fit between 10−4 and 10−5, and a bad fit for even higher values. The treated sample obtained a reasonable fit with a small χ2 at 10−5, and a marginal fit for the untreated sample. The stability of the treated sample as per the Kronig-Kramers’ results also proves the better photovoltaic performance obtained from the J-V measurement for the treated sample compared to the untreated sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance comparison with other types of GQDs-based DSSC
ce:section.ce:para: Performance characterization was conducted for different types of GQD-based DSSC using various sugar molecules such as cellulose (C-GQDs), glucose (G-GQDs), and fructose (F-GQDs). Sugar molecules were selected because they are abundantly available and environmentally friendly materials. These GQDs are compared using TiCl4 treatment with the untreated DSSC. In addition, CV and J-V characterizations were done to investigate the effect of TiCl4 treatment in DSSCs.

The CV curve of the TiCl4-treated GQDs-based DSSC devices compared with other types of GQDs is shown in Fig. 10 . From the current response in the CV curves, the current density of the device increased with the TiO2 photoanode treated with TiCl4 up to 245% (from 9.09 to 22.3 mA/cm2 at 0.8 V) for TiCl4-treated C-GQDs-based DSSC sample which indicating the treatment improves the electrical conductivity of the device performance [53].
Moreover, the CV shows a larger area suggesting the improvement of the electrocatalytic behavior of the device with the TiCl4-treated TiO2 photoanode [54].

Fig. 11 shows the J-V curves of other types of GQDs under TiCl4 treatment. Table 4 The J-V results revealed that all treated DSSCs have higher Jsc and Voc than the untreated DSSCs. The highest efficiency was obtained by cellulose-derived GQDs, followed by glucose and fructose. All the sugar molecules derived GQDs with TiCl4 treatment possessed greater Jsc than the untreated DSSCs. However, there is no effect on Voc after the treatment due to the same reason stated in Section 3.7.

Among the sugar molecules, cellulose exhibited the highest efficiency and fructose obtained the lowest efficiency. According to Rabeya et al., cellulose-derived GQDs have low structural defects, which may boost efficiency [38].

This work concluded that the chemically treated GQDs-based DSSCs exhibit improved electron transport properties than that without the chemical treatment. Photoluminescence-quenching study revealed that the treated sample exhibited the highest electron injection efficiency from the GQDS+N719 to TiO2/TiCl4 (ca. 73.8%). Accordingly, higher Jsc was observed for the treated sample indicating greater electron flow into the TiO2 electrode with a longer electron lifetime. Despite the promising electron injection efficiency (from GQDS+N719 to TiO2/TiCl4), the DSSC still suffers from poor photovoltaic conversion efficiency (PCE = 2%) due to the marginal stability observed from the Kronig-Kramers results. In addition, the EIS plots show high electron recombination at the photoanode/electrolyte interface for both treated and untreated samples. This event can be due to the poor fabrication of the DSSCs taken place without a sealant after the electrolyte injection in the laboratory. Therefore, we would suggest conducting DSSC fabrication by taking note of the electrolyte injection into the cell.

Savisha Mahalingam: Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation. Abreeza Manap: Funding acquisition, Supervision. Ramisha Rabeya: Writing – original draft, Software. Kam Sheng Lau: Writing – original draft, Software. Chin Hua Chia: Writing – review & editing. Huda Abdullah: Writing – review & editing. Nowshad Amin: Writing – review & editing. Puvaneswaran Chelvanathan: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Universiti Tenaga Nasional (UNITEN) for funding this study under the UNITEN BOLD of Grant No. J510050002/2022002.
